title: AuthGuard & Route Protection
description: Review Angular AuthGuard implementation, lifecycle hooks, NgRx patterns, and how Angular communicates with .NET Core APIs.
checklist: Explain AuthGuard and route access control
Describe all lifecycle hooks in Angular
Understand NgRx for state management
Explain Angular-to-.NET communication patterns
Understand two-way data binding and routing
answerHtml:
Sub QuestionAnswerMore ExplanationExampleBenefit
What is AuthGuard?A service that controls route access based on authenticationImplements CanActivate interface; returns boolean or Observable<boolean>Redirect unauthenticated users to login pageSecure routes from unauthorized access
How do you implement AuthGuard?Create service implementing CanActivate interfaceCheck authentication status in canActivate() methodAuthGuard checks JWT token validity before navigationControl route access declaratively
What is ngOnChanges?Lifecycle hook called when input properties changeReceives SimpleChanges object with previous/current valuesDetect when @Input() value updates and respondReact to external property changes
What is ngOnInit?Lifecycle hook called once after component initializationBest place for initialization logic and data fetchingLoad user profile data from API on component startInitialize component safely after inputs are bound
What is ngDoCheck?Lifecycle hook called during every change detection runImplement custom change detection logic hereDetect value changes that Angular's default detection missesImplement custom change detection strategies
What is ngAfterContentInit?Lifecycle hook called after ng-content is projectedInitialize logic that depends on projected contentAccess @ContentChild() after content is projectedInteract with content from parent component
What is ngAfterContentChecked?Lifecycle hook called after every check of ng-contentRuns after ngAfterContentInit and after ngDoCheckPerform additional checks after content initializationRespond to content projection changes
What is ngAfterViewInit?Lifecycle hook called after component view is initializedGood place to access DOM elements and child componentsInitialize jQuery plugins or access native DOMWork with view elements safely
What is ngAfterViewChecked?Lifecycle hook called after every check of component viewRuns after ngAfterViewInit and after change detectionPerform additional checks after view renderingUpdate UI elements after view changes
What is ngOnDestroy?Lifecycle hook called before component is destroyedEssential for cleanup and resource releaseUnsubscribe from observables, clear timers, detach event listenersPrevent memory leaks and cleanup resources
What is NgRx?Redux-inspired state management library for AngularManages application state predictably with immutable dataUse Store to hold user data, dispatch actions on loginCentralized state, easier debugging and testing
What are NgRx Actions?Plain objects representing events or user interactionsActions trigger reducers to update stateLoginAction, LogoutAction, FetchUserActionClear intent of what happened in the application
What are NgRx Reducers?Pure functions that specify state changes in response to actionsTake current state and action, return new stateReducer handles LoginAction by adding user to statePredictable state transitions, easy to test
What are NgRx Effects?Manage side effects like HTTP requests in NgRxListen for actions, perform async operations, dispatch new actionsEffect listens for FetchUserAction, calls API, dispatches LoadUserSuccessSeparate side effects from state management
What are NgRx Selectors?Functions to extract specific pieces of stateMemoized selectors prevent unnecessary component updatesselectUserName selector extracts user.name from storeEfficient state access and component optimization
How do Angular and .NET communicate?Angular uses HttpClient to send requests to .NET APIsCommunication over HTTP/HTTPS with JSON payloadsAngular calls GET /api/users/123 to .NET backendSeamless frontend-backend integration
What is HttpClient?Angular service for making HTTP requestsProvides methods for GET, POST, PUT, DELETE requeststhis.http.get<User>('/api/users')Simplified HTTP communication with observables
How do you handle errors in HTTP calls?Use error callbacks in subscribe() or catchError operator.NET backend provides HTTP status codes; Angular handles errorscatchError() maps 404 to user-friendly messageGraceful error handling and user feedback
What is two-way data binding?Automatic synchronization between model and view using ngModelChanges in view update model; model changes update view[(ngModel)]="user.name" creates two-way bindingReactive UI without manual DOM updates
What is Angular Routing?Client-side routing allowing navigation without page reloadsRouterModule handles URL changes and component switchingRoute to /dashboard or /settings without server requestSingle Page Application (SPA) experience
What is Dependency Injection (DI)?Components request dependencies rather than creating themInjector provides instances through constructor, property, or methodUserService injected into component constructorLoose coupling, easier testing with mock services
What are Angular Directives?Markers on DOM elements that attach Angular behaviorInclude structural (*ngIf, *ngFor) and attribute directives*ngIf="isLoggedIn" hides/shows element based on conditionDeclarative template logic and DOM manipulation
What is CORS?Cross-Origin Resource Sharing - policy for browser requestsConfigured on .NET backend to specify allowed origins.NET allows requests from http://localhost:4200Secure cross-origin API calls
How do you secure Angular-to-.NET communication?Use HTTPS, token-based auth (JWT), and permission validationBackend validates tokens on every requestStore JWT in localStorage, send in Authorization headerProtect sensitive data and user actions
How do you organize an Angular + .NET app?Separate frontend (Angular) and backend (.NET) codebasesOrganize by features/modules, separate concerns/angular-app for frontend, /dotnet-api for backendClear structure, easier maintenance and testing

title: Explain ARCHITECTURE & SYSTEM
description: Review architecture and system concepts for interview fluency.
checklist: Explain hybrid architecture
Describe monolith vs microservices
Summarize scalability and polymorphism in architecture
answerHtml:
Sub QuestionAnswerMore ExplanationExampleBenefit
Hybrid ArchitectureUI + API separationFrontend and backend separatedMVC + REST APIClean structure
Monolith vs MicroservicesOne app vs many servicesServices run independentlyPolicy, Claims, Billing separateScalability
ScalabilityHandle more loadAdd resources when neededClaim system during peak trafficPerformance
Compile vs Runtime PolymorphismOverloading vs OverridingCompile-time vs runtime decisionOverload / override methodsFlexibility
Singleton vs FactoryOne instance vs object creationSingleton reused, Factory creates objectsLogger vs PolicyFactoryControl objects

title: What is SOLID?
description: Review the SOLID principles, abstract/interface differences, and polymorphism for interview readiness.
checklist: Explain the SOLID principles in plain language
Describe Abstract class vs Interface
Summarize how polymorphism improves design
answerHtml:
Sub QuestionAnswerMore ExplanationExampleBenefit
What is SOLIDDesign principles for clean codeReduces complexity and future risksAdd new policy without breaking existing codeMaintainable system
SRP - Single ResponsibilityOne class = one jobAvoid mixed responsibilitiesPremiumCalculator only calculates premiumEasy testing
OCP - Open/ClosedExtend, don’t modifyAdd features without changing old codeAdd TravelPolicy without changing core logicSafer changes
LSP - Liskov SubstitutionChild behaves like parentNo broken behaviour when substitutingMotorPolicy works as PolicyReliable code
ISP - Interface Segregation PrincipleSmall focused interfacesDon’t force unused methodsIClaimable, IRenewable separatedCleaner design
DIP - Dependency InversionDepend on abstractionNot concrete classesClaimService uses IPaymentServiceFlexible + testable
Explain Abstract vs InterfaceAbstract = partial logic, Interface = contractAbstract has code, interface only rulesPolicy vs IClaimableFlexible design
Explain PolymorphismSame method, different behaviourBehaviour depends on objectCalculateClaim varies by policyReusable logic

title: Explain Design Patterns
description: Review common design patterns for interview-ready explanations.
checklist: Explain Singleton and Factory patterns
Describe Observer and reuse patterns
Summarize when to use these design patterns
answerHtml:
Sub QuestionAnswerMore ExplanationExampleBenefit
SingletonOne instance onlyGlobal shared objectLoggerSaves resources
FactoryCreate objects centrallyNo direct newPolicyFactoryClean code
ObserverNotify changesOne-to-many updateEmail alertsAuto updates

title: OOP Fundamentals
description: Review key object-oriented programming concepts with short definitions, explanations, examples, and benefits.
checklist: Explain the core OOP concepts clearly
Describe how objects, classes, and encapsulation relate
Summarize polymorphism, abstraction, and constructors
answerHtml:
Sub QuestionAnswerMore ExplanationExampleBenefit
What is Inheritance?Parent-child relationship of classesA subclass inherits properties and methods from its superclass for hierarchy and reuseclass Dog extends Animal {}Reduces duplication and supports extension
What is Polymorphism?One interface, many formsSame call can do different things depending on the object typeadd(int, int) / add(float) or Dog overrides sound()Makes code more flexible and reusable
What is an Object?Instance of a classObjects are concrete runtime values created from class blueprintsDog myDog = new Dog();Enables real-world modeling in code
What is a Class?Blueprint for objectsClasses define the properties and behavior that objects can haveclass Animal { int age; }Structures code and organizes behavior
What is Encapsulation?Protecting data by restricting accessHide state behind methods and control how it changesprivate int age; public int getAge()Improves security and prevents misuse
What is Abstraction?Hide implementation, show only essentialsSimplifies complex systems by exposing only required behaviorabstract class Shape { abstract void draw(); }Simplifies interfaces and reduces complexity
What is a Constructor?Initializes an object automaticallyCalled when an object is created to set up its initial statepublic Dog() { this.age = 1; }Ensures objects start with valid data
What is Method Overload?Same method name, different parametersMultiple methods share a name but accept different argumentsvoid run(int x) & void run(double y)Increases usability without new names
What is the Static keyword?Class-level member, not tied to any objectStatic members belong to the class itself and can be used without instancesstatic int counter;Saves memory and enables shared access
What is an Interface?Defines methods a class must implementInterfaces declare required behavior without implementationinterface Animal { void eat(); }Promotes consistent, testable design

title: Performance
description: Review Angular performance considerations, change detection, and when to optimize templates and observations.
checklist: Explain how change detection impacts Angular performance
Describe when to use OnPush change detection
Identify expensive template expressions to avoid
Summarize lazy loading and module splitting benefits
Mention the cost of large watch lists or observable subscriptions
answerHtml:
Sub QuestionAnswerMore ExplanationExampleBenefit
How does change detection affect performance?More checks mean more workAngular inspects bindings on each change detection cycleLarge component trees run slowerUnderstand render cost
When use OnPush?For immutable inputs and fewer checksComponent only checks when inputs change or events occur@Component({ changeDetection: ChangeDetectionStrategy.OnPush })Reduces unnecessary cycles
What are expensive template expressions?Functions or complex logic inside templatesThey run on every change detection pass{{ computeValue(item) }}Prefer pipes and memoized values
How does lazy loading help?Load modules only when neededReduces initial bundle size and startup cost{ path: 'admin', loadChildren: () => import('./admin/admin.module') }Faster initial load
Why limit subscriptions and watchers?Each one adds runtime overheadUnmanaged subscriptions can leak and slow the apptakeUntil(destroy$)More stable, efficient code

title: Dependency Injection Example
description: Implement a simple service and client class to demonstrate constructor dependency injection.
checklist: Create a Service class with an operation method
Create a Client class that accepts Service in the constructor
Store the injected service in a private readonly field
Call the service operation from the client
Return or use the service result
detailedInstructions: Step 1: Define a Service class with a PerformOperation method
Step 2: Define a Client class with a constructor that accepts Service
Step 3: Assign the injected service to a readonly field

title: Find Duplicate Records
description: Write a SQL query to identify duplicate rows in a table based on one or more columns.
checklist: Select the columns to check for duplicates
Use GROUP BY on those columns
Add HAVING COUNT(*) > 1 to filter duplicates
Return the duplicate values

Step 2: GROUP BY those same columns to group identical values together
Step 3: Use HAVING COUNT(*) > 1 to show only groups with more than 1 record
Step 4: This shows you which values appear multiple times
Step 5: You can also SELECT COUNT(*) to see how many times each appears

-- 1: SELECT the columns to check for duplicates
-- 2: GROUP BY those columns
-- 3: HAVING COUNT(*) > 1
-- 4: Return the duplicate values

GROUP BY those columns
HAVING COUNT(*) > 1
Return the duplicate values

SELECT column1, column2, COUNT(*)
FROM YourTable
GROUP BY column1, column2
HAVING COUNT(*) > 1;

title: Extract First Name

checklist: Use FullName column from EmployeeDetails
Extract the substring before the first space

title: Employees Who Are Managers

FROM EmployeeDetails

title: Min and Max Salary in One Query
description: Write a SQL query to display the employee with minimum and maximum salary in a single query.
checklist: Use subqueries or UNION to obtain min and max salary

-- 1: Use min and max salary in a single query
-- 2: Select employee name, position, salary

hints.guide: Select employee information for minimum salary
hints.code:
SELECT Name, Position, Salary
FROM employee
WHERE Salary = (SELECT MIN(Salary) FROM employee)

hints.guide: Select employee information for maximum salary
hints.code:
UNION
SELECT Name, Position, Salary
FROM employee
WHERE Salary = (SELECT MAX(Salary) FROM employee);

title: Remove Duplicate Records
description: Write a SQL query to remove duplicate rows from a table without using a temporary table.
checklist: Identify duplicate rows using a ranking or counting method
Keep one row per duplicate group
Remove rows with a higher duplicate rank
Avoid use of a temporary table
detailedInstructions: Step 1: Use a CTE or subquery to rank duplicate rows
Step 2: Assign row numbers within each duplicate group
Step 3: Delete rows where the rank is greater than 1
Step 4: Keep one unique row per duplicate set
starterCode:
-- 1: Use a CTE with ROW_NUMBER() to identify duplicates
-- 2: Delete rows with row number > 1
-- 3: Keep one unique row per group

hints.guide: Define a CTE to rank duplicate rows
hints.code:
WITH RankedData AS (
    SELECT EmpId, FullName, ManagerId, DateOfJoining,
           ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY FullName, ManagerId, DateOfJoining ORDER BY EmpId) AS RowNo
    FROM EmployeeDetails
)

hints.guide: Delete rows where row number is greater than 1

title: Salary Range Query
description: Write a SQL query to fetch employee names whose salary is between 5000 and 10000 inclusive.
checklist: Use EmployeeDetails and EmployeeSalary tables together
Join the tables using EmpId

Select the employee names
Return only matching employee records

Step 2: Add a salary filter using BETWEEN or comparisons

Step 4: Return the matching employee names

Join EmployeeSalary with EmployeeDetails by EmpId

FROM EmployeeDetails ED
JOIN EmployeeSalary ES ON ED.EmpId = ES.EmpId

hints.guide: Filter salary between 5000 and 10000
Return employee names for matching records
hints.code:
SELECT ED.FullName
FROM EmployeeDetails ED
JOIN EmployeeSalary ES ON ED.EmpId = ES.EmpId
WHERE ES.Salary >= 5000 AND ES.Salary <= 10000;

title: Second Largest Salary
description: Write a SQL query to fetch the second highest salary using ranking or OFFSET.
checklist: Query the employee salary values
Use RANK() or OFFSET to find the second highest salary
Return the second largest salary row

detailedInstructions: Step 1: Rank salaries in descending order
Step 2: Filter for rank = 2 or offset 1 row
Step 3: Return the second largest salary
Step 4: Use a single SQL query
starterCode:
-- 1: Use ranking or OFFSET to locate the second largest salary
-- 2: Return the salary value or row
-- 3: Keep the query in a single statement

hints.guide: Rank salaries in descending order
hints.code:
WITH RankedSalaries AS (
    SELECT EmployeeID, Salary, RANK() OVER (ORDER BY Salary DESC) AS SalaryRank
    FROM Employees
)

title: AuthGuard, Routing, and Authorization
description: Review Angular route protection, route configuration, and when to use guards for authorization.
checklist: Explain how AuthGuard uses CanActivate for protected routes
Show where to register guards in route definitions
Describe route parameter syntax and child routes
Discuss role-based or permission-based route decisions
Explain why auth logic belongs in a guard instead of a component
answerHtml:
Sub QuestionAnswerMore ExplanationExampleBenefit
What is AuthGuard?Route guard implementing CanActivateChecks auth state before route activation{ path: 'secure', canActivate: [AuthGuard] }Protects routes centrally
Where do you configure routes?In RouterModule.forRoot() or forChild()Defines path, component, children, and guardsconst routes = [{ path: 'admin', component: AdminComponent }];Central route configuration
How do you handle route params?Use :id in the path and ActivatedRouteSupports dynamic segments and nested routes{ path: 'users/:id', component: UserDetailComponent }Allows reusable route templates
How does authorization differ from authentication?Auth = identity, authorization = allowed actionsUsers can be authenticated but still restrictedAuthGuard checks roles or permissionsSafer application access control
Why keep auth logic in a guard?Separates security from UI componentsComponents stay focused on renderingGuard returns true/false or redirectsCleaner, maintainable routing security

title: Controller, $rootScope & Transclusion
description: Review AngularJS controllers, root scope sharing, and transclusion concepts for component-style templates.
checklist: Describe how controllers attach data to $scope
Explain the purpose of $rootScope
Summarize transclusion and how ng-transclude works
Compare controller scope and root scope visibility
Discuss why transclusion supports reusable templates
answerHtml:
Sub QuestionAnswerMore ExplanationExampleBenefit
What is an Angular controller?Function that manages scope dataAttaches properties and behavior to $scopefunction PolicyCtrl($scope) { $scope.policy = {}; }Organizes view model logic
What is $rootScope?Global scope shared across the appStores values accessible from any child scope$rootScope.currentUser = user;Useful for app-wide state
What is transclusion?Embedding original content into a directiveDirective template can include passed markup
Builds reusable wrappers
How does controller scope differ from $rootScope?Controller scope is local, rootScope is globalChild scopes inherit from $rootScope and can override values$scope.title = 'Policy';Limits scope pollution
Why use transclusion?Allows content projection into directivesComponents keep layout and content separateTextMore flexible UI composition

title: Digest Cycle
description: Explain Angular change detection and the digest cycle in a framework-agnostic special case.
checklist: Summarize the digest cycle and how Angular tracks model changes
Explain when change detection runs
Describe how $scope and $apply relate to digest
Compare manual versus automatic change detection
Mention performance implications for watch counts
answerHtml:
Sub QuestionAnswerMore ExplanationExampleBenefit
What is the digest cycle?AngularJS change detection loopIt checks watched expressions and updates bindings$scope.$digest()Keeps UI in sync
When does change detection run?After event handlers and async callbacksAngularJS runs digest after model changes in the zone$scope.$apply()Ensures model and view stay consistent
What is $scope?Scope object for binding contextHolds model data and watches for changes$scope.title = 'Policy';Organizes template data
What is $apply?Wraps code to trigger digestEnsures external changes are detected$scope.$apply(() => { ... });Makes non-Angular changes visible
Why does digest count matter?More watches can slow down the appKeep watch expressions simple and few$scope.$watch('policy.name', ...)Better performance

title: Secure Communication
description: Review HTTPS, CORS configuration, and API security for Angular + .NET apps.
checklist: Explain why HTTPS is required for secure data exchange
Describe how CORS is configured in .NET Core
Show how Angular calls an API over a secure connection
Mention the role of authentication tokens or cookies
Summarize why backend and frontend must agree on security settings
answerHtml:
Sub QuestionAnswerMore ExplanationExampleBenefit
Why use HTTPS?Encrypts data over the wirePrevents eavesdropping and tamperinghttps://api.example.comProtects sensitive information
What is CORS?Browser policy for cross-origin requestsServer must explicitly allow trusted originsapp.UseCors("AllowSpecificOrigins")Prevents unauthorized API access
How does Angular call a secure API?Use HttpClient with https endpointBackend should serve TLS and return CORS headersthis.http.get('https://api.example.com') Safe front-end/back-end communication
What are auth tokens?Credentials sent with requestsJWT or cookies prove user identity to the APIAuthorization: Bearer tokenSecures each request
Why align backend and frontend security?Both sides must trust and authenticate requestsAPI restrictions and origin rules must match client behaviorAllow origins + secure headersReliable end-to-end security

title: Unit Testing
description: Review Angular unit testing with TestBed, mock services, and component assertions.
checklist: Explain TestBed configuration for a component test
Describe how to mock a service dependency
Write an expectation for component rendering
Use async utilities for observable or promise-based code
Explain the benefit of isolated unit tests
answerHtml:
Sub QuestionAnswerMore ExplanationExampleBenefit
What is TestBed?Angular testing module builderSets up component fixtures and providersTestBed.configureTestingModule({ declarations: [MyComponent] })Creates isolated test environments
How do you mock a service?Create a fake providerReplace real dependency with test double{ provide: DataService, useValue: mockService }Tests stay stable and focused
How do you assert rendered output?Query fixture.nativeElementInspect DOM after change detectionexpect(element.textContent).toContain('Policy')Verifies UI behavior
Why use async utilities?Handle observables and promises in testsMakes async code deterministicwaitForAsync(() => { ... })Prevents flaky tests
Why isolate unit tests?Focus on one component or serviceAvoid dependency on external systemsmock providers and simple fixturesFaster, more reliable feedback

title: React.js Core Concepts
description: Interview-ready flashcards covering React fundamentals: declarative UI, components, virtual DOM, JSX, hooks, state management, lifecycle, and best practices.
checklist: Understand React's declarative approach and component architecture
Explain Virtual DOM and reconciliation process
Describe state, props, and their differences
Understand lifecycle methods and React Hooks
Explain context API and state management patterns
Understand JSX, Babel, and modern React workflows
answerHtml:
Sub QuestionAnswerMore ExplanationExampleBenefit
What is React?A JavaScript library for building user interfaces with componentsReact focuses on creating reusable, self-contained pieces of UIBuild dashboard with Navbar, Sidebar, MainContent componentsReusable, maintainable code
What is declarative UI?You describe the desired UI state; React handles DOM updatesEasier to understand and debug than imperative codeWrite JSX describing the UI, React updates DOM automaticallyCleaner, more readable code
What is component-based architecture?React apps are built using reusable, self-contained componentsEach component manages its own state and logicUserCard, ProductList, SearchBar are individual componentsModularity and code reuse
What is the Virtual DOM?An in-memory representation of the actual DOMReact calculates minimal updates needed and applies them efficientlyReact compares virtual DOM tree, identifies differences, updates real DOMOptimized rendering performance
How does Virtual DOM reconciliation work?React compares old and new virtual DOM trees (diffing) and updates only necessary partsMinimizes direct manipulations to real DOM for efficiencyList items change: React updates only modified items, not the entire listBetter performance and user experience
What is JSX?JavaScript XML - a syntax extension allowing HTML-like syntax in JavaScriptJSX simplifies creation of React elements and improves readability<div className="card"><h1>{title}</h1></div>More readable and maintainable code
How is JSX transformed?Babel transpiles JSX into JavaScript function calls (React.createElement)Browser doesn't understand JSX, so Babel converts it to compatible code<Button onClick={handleClick}/> becomes React.createElement(Button, {onClick: handleClick})Write cleaner code, run anywhere
What is unidirectional data flow?Data flows in one direction: parent to child through propsMaintains predictable state and makes changes easier to trackParent: <Child data={value}/>; Child receives and uses dataPredictable, easier to debug
What is the difference between state and props?State: internal, mutable data; Props: external, immutable data from parentState causes re-render when changed; props are read-only in childState: this.state = {count: 0}; Props: <Counter initialValue={5}/>Clear data management boundaries
Can you modify props in a child component?No, props are immutable and read-only in child componentsTo update, call a callback function passed by parentParent: <Child onUpdate={setName}/>; Child: onClick={() => onUpdate(newValue)}Prevents unintended data mutations
What are lifecycle methods?Methods called at different stages of component existence: mounting, updating, unmountingAllow you to run code at specific times in component lifecyclecomponentDidMount: fetch data; componentWillUnmount: cleanup subscriptionsControl component behavior at key moments
What is componentDidMount()?Lifecycle method called after component is rendered on screenGood place for network requests, timers, and subscriptionsFetch user data from API when component loadsExecute setup logic after component ready
What is componentDidUpdate()?Lifecycle method invoked after component updatesReceives previous props and state; useful for side effectsUpdate page title when props change, log state updatesRespond to prop/state changes after rendering
What is componentWillUnmount()?Lifecycle method called before component is removed from DOMEssential for cleanup: cancel requests, unsubscribe, clear timersUnsubscribe from socket, cancel API requests, clear intervalsPrevent memory leaks
What are React Hooks?Functions allowing functional components to have state and lifecycle methodsIntroduced in React 16.8; alternative to class componentsuseState for state, useEffect for side effects, useContext for contextSimpler, cleaner code; easier to reuse logic
What is useState()?Hook for adding state to functional componentsReturns [value, setter] pair; setter updates state and triggers re-renderconst [count, setCount] = useState(0); then setCount(count + 1)Simple state management in functional components
What is useEffect()?Hook for performing side effects in functional componentsRuns after render; replaces componentDidMount, componentDidUpdate, componentWillUnmountuseEffect(() => { fetchData(); }, []); fetch runs once on mountClean up effects; avoid memory leaks
What is the dependency array in useEffect?Array of dependencies; effect runs when dependencies changeEmpty array: runs once on mount; no array: runs after every render; with deps: runs when deps changeuseEffect(() => { fetch(userId); }, [userId]); runs when userId changesControl when effects run; optimize performance
What is useContext()?Hook to consume context values without prop drillingAllows components to access shared state directly from Contextconst theme = useContext(ThemeContext); use theme.color directlyAvoid prop drilling through multiple levels
What is useMemo()?Hook to memoize computation results; prevents unnecessary recalculationsReturns memoized value; recalculates only when dependencies changeconst expensiveValue = useMemo(() => complexCalculation(data), [data])Optimize performance for expensive operations
What is useCallback()?Hook to memoize function; prevents unnecessary function re-creationReturns memoized callback; useful for passing to optimized child componentsconst handleClick = useCallback(() => { doSomething(); }, [dependency])Prevent child re-renders caused by function prop changes
What is useRef()?Hook creating mutable reference that persists across rendersCommon use: access DOM elements directly, store mutable valuesconst inputRef = useRef(null); then inputRef.current.focus()Direct DOM access; persist values without causing re-renders
What is the Context API?Built-in React feature allowing components to share state without prop drillingCreate context, wrap tree with provider, consume with useContext or ConsumerCreate ThemeContext, pass theme through Provider to all descendantsManage global state simply without external libraries
What is prop drilling?Passing props through multiple intermediate components to reach target componentBecomes unwieldy with deeply nested componentsApp → Page → Section → Card → Button receives prop from AppContext API and state management libraries solve this
How do you handle forms in React?Use state to manage input values; handle onChange and onSubmit eventsControlled components use state as source of truthconst [email, setEmail] = useState(''); <input value={email} onChange={(e) => setEmail(e.target.value)} />Controlled inputs; easy validation and submission
What is a controlled component?Component whose value is controlled by React stateForm input value comes from state; onChange updates stateInput value displays state; typing updates state; state updates inputFull control over input behavior
What is an uncontrolled component?Component managing its own state internally; accessed via refDOM is source of truth; less common in Reactconst nameRef = useRef(); then access nameRef.current.value in handlerSimpler for simple inputs; less React integration
How does React handle events?Use camelCase event handlers (onClick, onChange, onSubmit)React uses event delegation; handlers are synthetic events<button onClick={handleClick}>Click</button>Consistent cross-browser event handling
What are keys and why are they important in lists?Unique identifiers for list items helping React recognize which items changedKeys improve performance and maintain component state in dynamic lists<li key={item.id}>{item.name}</li> instead of key={index}Correct rendering and state management in lists
Why shouldn't you use array index as key?If list reorders, index no longer matches the same item, breaking component stateComponent state gets tied to wrong item; causes bugs and performance issuesReorder list: index 0 now refers to different item; state gets mixed upUse unique IDs as keys
What is React.memo?Higher-order component preventing re-render if props haven't changedOptimizes performance by skipping render for unchanged propsexport default React.memo(MyComponent); skips render if props sameReduce unnecessary re-renders
What is a higher-order component (HOC)?Function taking component and returning enhanced componentPattern for code reuse, logic abstraction, cross-cutting concernswithTheme(Component) wraps component providing theme propsReuse logic across multiple components
What is render props pattern?Pattern where component accepts function as prop to render contentAlternative to HOC for sharing component logic<DataProvider render={data => <Component data={data} />} />Flexible component composition
How do you communicate React frontend with .NET Core backend?Use HTTP requests (fetch or Axios) to call REST APIs on backendReact components make requests; backend returns JSON datafetch('/api/users').then(res => res.json()).then(data => setUsers(data))Seamless frontend-backend integration
What is Axios?Popular HTTP client library for making API requestsCleaner syntax than fetch; better error handling; automatic transformationsaxios.get('/api/data').then(res => setData(res.data))Simpler HTTP handling
How do you handle errors in API calls?Use try-catch with async/await or .catch() with promisesGracefully handle network errors, timeouts, server errorscatch(error => setError(error.message)); display error to userBetter user experience
What is Redux?Predictable state container for JavaScript apps; manages centralized app stateStores entire state in single store; dispatch actions to trigger state changesStore contains user, posts, ui state; dispatch({type: 'SET_USER', payload})Predictable state management for complex apps
What are Redux actions?Plain objects describing state changes; must have type propertyOther properties carry data (payload) needed for state update{type: 'ADD_TODO', payload: {id: 1, text: 'Learn Redux'}}Clear intent of state changes
What are Redux reducers?Pure functions taking current state and action, returning new stateMust be pure; no side effects; always return new state(state = [], action) => action.type === 'ADD_TODO' ? [...state, action.payload] : statePredictable state transitions
What is useSelector in Redux?Hook to select state from Redux store in functional componentsEfficiently subscribes to store; re-renders only when selected state changesconst todos = useSelector(state => state.todos)Access Redux state cleanly in components
What is useDispatch in Redux?Hook to dispatch actions from functional componentsReturns dispatch function to trigger state changesconst dispatch = useDispatch(); dispatch(addTodo(newTodo))Trigger state changes from components
What is React Router?Library for client-side routing in React SPAsEnables navigation between pages without full page reloads<BrowserRouter> <Route path="/home" component={Home} /> </BrowserRouter>Seamless single-page app navigation
What is the difference between SSR and CSR?SSR: renders components on server, sends HTML to client; CSR: renders on client with JavaScriptSSR better for SEO and initial load; CSR better for user interactionsSSR: Next.js renders page on server; CSR: React renders page in browserChoose based on app needs
What is code splitting?Breaking app into smaller chunks loaded on demand instead of one big bundleImproves initial load performance; chunks loaded when neededReact.lazy() + Suspense for component splitting; Webpack dynamic importsFaster initial page load
What is lazy loading?Deferring loading of components until they're neededImproves performance; reduces initial bundle sizeconst Home = React.lazy(() => import('./Home')); wrapped in SuspenseBetter performance
What is Webpack?Module bundler combining JavaScript, CSS, images into optimized bundlesHandles code splitting, minification, asset managementWebpack bundles all modules into dist/bundle.jsOptimized production builds
What is Babel?JavaScript compiler transpiling modern ES6+ and JSX into browser-compatible codeEnables use of latest features while supporting older browsersBabel transforms <Component /> JSX into React.createElement(Component)Write modern code; run everywhere
What is TypeScript in React?Adds static typing to JavaScript; catches errors during developmentBetter tooling support, autocomplete, early error detectioninterface User { name: string; age: number; }; const user: User = {}Fewer bugs; better developer experience
What are custom hooks?Functions creating reusable stateful logic extracted from componentsCustom hooks are JavaScript functions starting with 'use'function useFetch(url) { const [data, setData] = useState(null); useEffect(() => {...}); return data; }Reuse complex logic across components
What is the useReducer hook?Hook for complex state logic; manages state with reducer functionBetter than useState for related state pieces; similar to Redux reducersconst [state, dispatch] = useReducer(reducer, initialState); dispatch({type: 'ACTION'})Manage complex state cleanly
What is CORS?Cross-Origin Resource Sharing - policy controlling cross-domain requests.NET Core backend must allow requests from React frontend domain.NET configure CORS: app.UseCors(builder => builder.AllowAnyOrigin())Secure yet flexible API access
How do you secure React-to-.NET communication?Use HTTPS, JWT tokens for authentication, validate permissions on backendStore JWT in localStorage/cookies; send in Authorization headerPOST /auth/login returns JWT; subsequent requests include token headerProtect sensitive data and user actions
What is authentication in React?Process of verifying user identity using credentials (username/password, OAuth, JWT)Backend validates credentials; returns token React stores and usesLogin: send credentials to backend; backend returns JWT tokenSecure user access
What is authorization in React?Process of determining what authenticated user can access/doBackend checks user roles/permissions; React conditionally renders based on rolesAdmin sees delete button; user doesn't; check backend permissionsControl user permissions
What is responsive design in React?Design approach making apps work on all device sizesUse CSS media queries, flexible layouts, and viewport meta tagCSS: @media (max-width: 768px) { .sidebar { display: none; } }Works on desktop, tablet, mobile
What is Bootstrap?CSS framework providing pre-styled components and responsive gridIncludes components like navbars, modals, buttons; responsive utilities<div className="container"> <div className="row"> <div className="col-md-6">Faster UI development
What is Material UI?React UI framework following Google Material Design principlesProvides React components with Material Design look and feelimport { Button, Card } from '@mui/material'; <Button variant="contained">Professional, consistent UI
What is performance optimization in React?Techniques reducing rendering, improving load time, and user experienceCode splitting, memoization, lazy loading, efficient state managementReact.memo prevents unnecessary renders; useMemo caches computationsFaster app, better UX
